skill.name: data-visualization
skill.description: Guidance for selecting, designing, and generating clear data visualizations with chart recipes, color rules, labels, and storytelling patterns.
skill.summary: Create clearer charts with practical visualization rules and inference.sh examples.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, blocker, and reconnaissance patterns. Most blocker matches are false positives from markdown formatting, color text, or checksum discussion, but the skill does instruct users to pipe a remote install script into a shell and to run code through a third-party CLI. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, but publication should require removal or hardening of the remote install and execution guidance.

high_findings.title: Remote Install Script Piped to Shell
high_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The quick start instructs users to download a script from a remote URL and execute it directly with the shell. This bypasses normal package review and can execute arbitrary installer code before the user can inspect it.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell pattern is present in an installation command. It is a well-known high-risk pattern even though malicious intent is not confirmed.

high_findings.title: Third-Party Remote Code Execution Workflow
high_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: Multiple examples submit Python or HTML payloads to inference.sh apps through the infsh CLI. This may be legitimate for the skill purpose, but users could expose sensitive data or code to a third-party service when adapting the examples.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly invoke remote app execution through a CLI. The risk depends on user inputs, so the finding is high confidence for exposure risk but not for malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Allowed Tool Enables External CLI Calls
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill grants Bash access for infsh commands. The scope is narrower than unrestricted shell access, but it still allows external command execution and network-backed workflows through the CLI.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration is explicit and limited to infsh. This confirms an external-command surface, but the restriction reduces command injection concern.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Service URLs
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The document links to inference.sh, an install script URL, and checksum information. These URLs are expected for the CLI workflow, but they create dependency on external network resources.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network locations are directly visible and match the skill purpose. They are not evidence of exfiltration by themselves.

low_findings.title: Static Blocker Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The weak-cryptography and system-reconnaissance findings appear to come from ordinary prose, chart text, markdown examples, or checksum discussion. No evidence of MD5, SHA-1 use, host fingerprinting, or malicious reconnaissance was found in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found benign markdown and visualization guidance at these locations. There is no semantic evidence of weak cryptography or reconnaissance behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Detections Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The Ruby or shell backtick detections are caused by markdown code fences and inline command formatting, not Ruby execution syntax. Some fenced examples contain real CLI commands, which are covered by separate findings.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are markdown fences or inline code in SKILL.md. They do not represent Ruby backtick execution by the skill file itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: curl to shell installer
dangerous_patterns.description: A remote installer is piped directly to sh. Require manual download, signature verification, or a package-manager install before publication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command directly combines curl output with shell execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote chart generation through CLI apps
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples send executable chart-generation payloads to inference.sh apps. The skill should warn users not to include private data or secrets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The workflow explicitly runs user-supplied code through named infsh apps.

content.value_statement: Teams often choose charts that hide the real message or mislead readers. This skill gives practical chart selection, design, labeling, and storytelling guidance for clearer visual reports.
actual_capabilities: Recommends chart types for time series, comparisons, distributions, rankings, heatmaps, and composition data.
Provides practical rules for axes, labels, legends, sorting, and chart titles.
Includes color palette guidance for sequential, diverging, categorical, and colorblind-safe charts.
Shows example workflows for bar charts, line charts, heatmaps, KPI cards, and dark mode charts.
Explains how to use annotations and insight-focused titles to tell a data story.
limitations: The examples depend on the inference.sh CLI and external app execution.
It does not validate the correctness or quality of the user supplied data.
It gives general visualization guidance, not domain-specific statistical analysis.
The included examples are templates and may need adaptation for production dashboards.

use_cases.title: Executive Report Charts
use_cases.description: Turn revenue, acquisition, or retention data into clear charts with concise titles and useful annotations.

use_cases.title: Product Dashboard Design
use_cases.description: Choose readable chart types for usage trends, funnels, cohorts, and KPI summaries.

use_cases.title: Presentation Visual Cleanup
use_cases.description: Replace cluttered visuals with simpler charts, stronger labels, and accessible color choices.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Chart
prompt_templates.prompt: I have data about [describe columns and goal]. Recommend the best chart type and explain why it fits.

prompt_templates.title: Improve a Chart
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this chart idea: [describe chart]. Suggest better axes, labels, colors, and title text.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Dashboard Section
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a dashboard section for [audience] using these metrics: [metrics]. Recommend chart types, ordering, and annotations.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Data Story
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a data story from this trend: [summary]. Define the context, tension, resolution, chart sequence, and key callouts.

output_examples.input: Monthly revenue by product line with one product driving most growth.
output_examples.output: A stacked or grouped bar chart recommendation, an insight title, direct labels, and one annotation for the growth driver.

output_examples.input: Website traffic by weekday and hour.
output_examples.output: A heatmap plan with readable labels, a sequential palette, peak-hour annotations, and a short dashboard caption.

output_examples.input: A slide with a pie chart showing eight acquisition channels.
output_examples.output: A horizontal bar chart replacement with sorted values, limited colors, direct labels, and a clearer takeaway title.

best_practices: Start by naming the decision or message the chart must support.
Use the simplest chart type that makes the comparison clear.
Label important values directly when precision or emphasis matters.
anti_patterns: Do not use pie charts for many categories or small differences.
Do not truncate bar chart axes because it exaggerates differences.
Do not use color as the only way to encode important meaning.

faq.question: Does this skill generate chart files directly?
faq.answer: It includes example commands for generating chart images with inference.sh, but the main value is visualization guidance.

faq.question: Can I use it without inference.sh?
faq.answer: Yes. The chart selection, design rules, color advice, and storytelling guidance can be applied in any charting tool.

faq.question: What chart types does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers bar charts, line charts, scatter plots, heatmaps, KPI cards, stacked charts, treemaps, and maps.

faq.question: Does it support accessibility?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends colorblind-safe palettes, direct labels, readable text sizes, and avoiding red or green as the only signal.

faq.question: Is it safe for private data?
faq.answer: Be careful. The included CLI examples may send code or data to an external service, so avoid private or regulated data.

faq.question: Can it help with dashboards?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide chart choice, metric layout, titles, annotations, and visual consistency for dashboard sections.
